statement: Finland and Sweden secured permanent national agricultural aid provisions under Article 142 of the 1994 Act of Accession. This allows them to grant long-term national aid to maintain agricultural activity in northern regions with harsh climatic conditions, low population density (<10 persons/km²), and limited agricultural area (<10% of total land). Finland's Article 142 aid totals approximately €290 million per year (roughly 90% of its total national agricultural aid), covering per-animal payments for cattle, sheep, and horses, per-litre payments for milk, hectare-based crop payments, and greenhouse horticulture support. The current authorisation (2022–2027) caps total aid at €574.5 million per year, with a maximum of €221 million for cow's milk. Sweden has a parallel scheme. Austria received separate transitional and structural aid provisions. These are the closest precedents to what Iceland might negotiate.
source_name: Act of Accession of Austria, Finland and Sweden (1994), Article 142; Finnish Ministry of Agriculture and Forestry; Commission Decision (EU) 2022/2460
caveats: Article 142 aid is not a 'permanent derogation from the CAP' — it supplements CAP payments rather than replacing them. Finland and Sweden still fully implement the CAP; the national aid is additional. The aid must not lead to increased production or exceed pre-accession support levels. Whether Iceland could negotiate similar provisions depends on its accession terms, and no two accessions are identical.

related_entries.statement: Icelandic agricultural subsidies totalled approximately ISK 20–22 billion (roughly €130–145 million) per year as of 2024, primarily through direct payments to sheep and dairy farmers. This equates to approximately 55–60% of gross farm revenue being derived from government support, making Iceland one of the most heavily subsidised agricultural sectors in the OECD. The OECD Producer Support Estimate (PSE) for Iceland was 54% in 2023, compared to the EU average of approximately 19%.

Norway, the closest comparator to Iceland as an EEA EFTA state with a protected agricultural sector, spends approximately NOK 20 billion (roughly €1.7 billion) per year on agricultural subsidies and maintains tariffs of 100–400% on key products (dairy, meat). Norway's OECD Producer Support Estimate is approximately 55%, similar to Iceland's. Despite not being in the EU, Norway has gradually liberalised some agricultural trade through bilateral agreements with the EU (Article 19 reviews under the EEA Agreement).

Norway held EU membership referendums in 1972 (on EEC membership) and 1994 (on EU membership), both resulting in narrow 'No' votes: 53.5% No in 1972 and 52.2% No in 1994. Key issues included fisheries, agriculture, national sovereignty, and regional policy. Urban areas voted Yes while rural and northern areas voted No.

citations.report_title: 390.000 hektarar af landbúnaðarlandi breytast í skóg og votlendi

claims.claim_text: Í Finnlandi hefur stuðningur úr sameiginlegu landbúnaðarstefnunni verið nýttur til að greiða bændum fyrir að breyta landnotkun á kolefnisríkum jarðvegi og endurheimta votlendi.

citations.report_title: Enginn afsláttur gefinn segi þjóðin já við aðildarviðræðum

claims.claim_text: Þegar Svíþjóð og Finnland gengu í Evrópusambandið var búin til sérstök landbúnaðarstefna um stöðu þeirra landa.

citations.report_title: Landbúnaðarstefna ESB henti íslenskum landbúnaði illa

claims.claim_text: Sameiginleg landbúnaðarstefna ESB (CAP) hentar afar illa hér á landi.
Engar varanlegar almennar undanþágur frá sameiginlegri landbúnaðarstefnu ESB hafa verið veittar.
Undanþágur frá landbúnaðarstefnu ESB hafa einungis verið veittar tímabundið, aldrei varanlega.
Ekki er hægt að fá varanlegar undanþágur frá sameiginlegri landbúnaðarstefnu ESB.
